title: Influences, Traces, Tribes, and Perhaps Also Thresholds
speaker: Gil Kalai
abstract: I will describe some recent results and problems regarding influence of sets of variables on Boolean functions: In 1989 Benny Chor conjectured that a balanced Boolean function with n variables has a subset S of size 0.4n with influence (1-c^n) where c0 follows from a theorem by Kahn, Kalai and Linial (KKL).I will present a recent counterexample by Kahn and me showing that up to the identity of c, the KKL bound cannot be improved.

proof_stages.label: Boolean functions and set influences
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the discrete cube, Boolean functions, monotonicity, individual influences, Ben-Or--Linial influences of sets toward 1 and 0, and the shifting reduction to monotone Boolean functions when minimizing influences.

proof_stages.label: KKL baseline and iterated influence bounds
proof_stages.summary: The Kahn--Kalai--Linial theorem gives a variable of influence at least on the order of mu(f)(1-mu(f)) log n/n, and iterating this individual-variable result yields polynomially close-to-total influence for linear-size sets.

proof_stages.label: Chor-type conjectures and their disproval
proof_stages.summary: Chor's conjecture and a related small-measure conjecture predicted much stronger large-set influence, but the new work with Kahn constructs counterexamples showing that the iterative KKL bounds are essentially sharp in broad regimes.

proof_stages.label: Tribes, dual tribes, and Harper limitations
proof_stages.summary: Standard and dual tribes, together with Harper's edge-isoperimetric theorem and modified tribe sizes, explain the surprising KKL logarithm and show that improving individual-variable influence bounds cannot prove the desired large-set conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Earlier large-set obstructions and bounded-depth structure
proof_stages.summary: Prior examples of Ajtai--Linial type use literals, depth-three circuits, and randomness to obstruct simultaneous influence toward 1 and toward 0, while bounded-depth circuit theorems explain why such circuit constructions have controlled total influence.

proof_stages.label: Traces, shattering, and the influence reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk translates influence questions into trace and almost-shattering questions for set families, reviewing the Sauer--Shelah lemma, its induction proof, arrow notation, and the equivalence between large influence and almost complete traces.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the Hamming-ball trace intuition
proof_stages.summary: Bollobas--Radcliffe and later Sharon--Shelah--Kalai counterexamples show that Hamming balls do not control trace extremals, even for almost shattering, thereby preparing the ground for the new negative examples.

proof_stages.label: New random literal dual-tribes counterexamples and boundary questions
proof_stages.summary: The new Kahn--Kalai construction uses random overlapping depth-two dual tribes built from literals to refute the large-set influence conjectures, after which the talk formulates remaining boundary problems and related random-graph threshold conjectures.

examples.name: Planar graph edge bound and the R^4 two-complex analogue
examples.note: The opening aside compares Euler's planar-graph bound e <= 3v with a conjectural F <= 4E bound for 2-dimensional simplicial complexes embedded in R^4, but it is not used in the Boolean-function argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the planar edge bound and the R^4 two-complex conjectural analogue are stated as motivating inequalities, without proving or applying them

examples.engagement_reason: random graph connectivity is concretely translated into the no-isolated-vertices condition and identified structurally with a dual-tribes setup using vertex edge-neighborhoods as tribes

examples.name: Random-graph threshold benchmarks such as Hamiltonian cycles
examples.note: Subgraph-containment and Hamiltonian-cycle thresholds illustrate the final conjectural link between influence questions, easy first-moment threshold parameters, and possible logarithmic gaps.
